item.title: Staff Post - Info
item.content:encoded:
Noting that a recent series of illegitimate tags needed to be removed from this article.

Author, if you are not familiar with the tagging system, then allow staff to add tags for you. All of your recent tag additions have required staff intervention. Do not continue to do this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That… is unexpectedly good. I mean, really good, the escalation has the right pacing, the narrative is steady…
Definitely +1

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Duly noted. The removed image has been replaced with one roughly approximating the original, but using public domain images as the base, which can be found here:

https://publicdomainreview.org/collections/hi-res-images-from-the-apollo-missions/

https://skyrisecities.com/news/2016/07/cityscape-googie-go-go-la-las-vegas-part-ii

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license. The base image comes from here, and is owned by the Chinese news company Xinhua. With no indication the image has been released under Creative Commons, the image has been removed.

EDIT: The image has been replaced and is cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As mentioned in some of the more recent posts, I feel that this is an entirely different version of the SCP than the one I read. I enjoyed this SCP; it has a simple yet interesting core concept (a replica hotel on the moon), believable and feasible containment (suppression of public knowledge), and offers some insight to what happened to the occupants and the hotel itself, while leaving it a mystery as to the actual cause of the anomaly's creation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was this influenced by the Star Trek Next Generation episode Hotel Royale by any chance?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I haven't read the previous versions, but I really like what it's been reworked into.

Unexplained (as in not over-explained, which is great) hotel duplication on the moon, compelling diary entries for a backstory, upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get the sense that the previous versions of this article were very different. The one I just read is great. Really loved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When in doubt, do a little and do it well. The idea of a hotel spontaneously ending up on the moon and the journals detailing the final days of the people left in it were done perfectly. If I had to give a reason for why it's up there (and believe me, you absolutely don't have to. The core idea feels strong enough to work on its own)… I'd make it fit with the Vegas of that time period. Maybe a type green was performing as a magician and made the hotel disappear without any solid plan to bring it back. Maybe the alien mafia got irritated and chose to repossess their property… then decided it was too much hassle to keep the casino so they dropped it back off in more or less the right area. It doesn't necessarily have to be corny and over the top, it also doesn't have to NOT be corny and over the top, either, it just has to mesh well with what's already established in the rest of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I were to rework this, I'd retool the article by focusing on the SCPF piecing together everything as they record it.:

+ show block
– hide block

So, say, first addendum would be a recording of the state and nature of all the bodies they find, and allowing the events to be inferred from that.

  • Several people found outside the atmospheric safe-zone, with small details that suggest what they were up to/how they attempted to escape.
  • Cannibalized or mutilated remains in the game area
  • Some poor sod that locked themselves up in the vault and died alone
  • Someone of importance strung up and brutalized or crucified
  • Some of the hired goons something something they ded

etc. etc. etc. Basically, think about the arrangement of these things, or other small details about the hotel itself, that build an outline juuust around the happenings therein. A gentle push in the right direction would be more effective (to me) than a hard shove.

Flowing from that, I would maybe follow that with more disparate logs: crude graffiti scrawled in a certain area, a snippet of an account from someone who can only imagine what's actually going on, a suicide note from a gambler…hell, even some of the diary you've got right now might work out just fine. But again, building around rather than filling completely in.

For -1? I'm not entirely sure what to do with that. Something can come from it, or rather, it can have a place, I suppose, but involving Stock Bad Guis I feel was a poor choice.

Point is, you don't really need it, unless I suppose you need it to be there just to kill everyone else off (it's just a plot point). If that's the case, I'd say it's interchangeable, and I'm certain with some brainstorming you could find something more evocative or alluring or simply just Dark that could serve just as well in that capacity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So axe the Chaos Insurgency aspect completely, along with the constant containment, and present the post-duplication hotel as is? Or would that make the article a tad plain?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So I should just axe the final two addenda completely? I feel like without the generator the article would be a bit plain… any pointers for how I could fix those aspects instead of removing them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Diary entries weren't half bad, but it's a common enough vehicle for exposition that it sticks out as just that - and it's really hard to get into unless they're executed particularly well.

They're not really bad per se, but they're too matter-of-fact to be believable (especially considering how it's even recognized in the work: "I’m writing now for anyone who’ll listen. Anyone who stumbles across this record and wants to know what happened here.").

The story you have there? It's good. Like, everything about the dissolving situation, culminating in survivors gambling for their lives? Fuck. I'd eat that shit up - just not a fan of how this is executed.

This was off to a strong start, I think; it opens up with a compelling tableau. I believe this would have been more interesting with a less-is-more approach: present (more vividly) the state of things as they are discovered after the fact, rather than give a primer, and explain everything with a conveniently-thorough diary.

The CI stuff is what cinched my downvote. Of all the possible things to have happened involving the story you've built, it seems like the least interesting angle to follow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Third'd

The diary was a strong point, everything after that just feels unnecessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea of the journal entries, to a degree. The whole descent into madness by the hotel director feels earnest and believable (if a bit hamfisted at the end with the whole "preservation of dignity" deal), and all told this feels like something out of a Fallout game. I thoroughly enjoyed that portion of the article. That's the good.

The bad is the anomalous generator. I'm feeling like there's a serious disconnect between a generator that just provides enough power for anything that's hooked up to it and BOOM! Hotel on the motherf**kin moon!

The concepts don't flow well into each other at all and completely jar me out of the whole "Did they piss off the alien mafia?!" deal I was enjoying up until that point. My advice would be to lose the generator completely or find some way to bridge that gap between the anomaly and the effect. Because right now they feel like two separate stories that are competing for my attention while I read this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm getting a tad disheartened, purely because this draft was already written to specifically address and fix many of the criticisms I got from PMs.

Like you criticised the vague nature of SCP-3655-1, so I tried to explain it and its allure in the secure document.

You criticised the unbelievable nature of the director even making diary entries in such a situation, so I rewrote the whole thing to try and make him more sympathetic, as well as implying he's writing the entries for his wife/lover.

I just don't see how it could really be improved without drastic measures… maybe replace the diary entries with an initial exploration log?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any pointers for improving it? It's just this draft was already completely rewritten from an earlier one, with the intent to fix ome of MrWrong's criticisms. I'm sort of at a loss.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding wrong's feelings above.

This started off actually pretty strong, I was like 'Wtf, hotel on the moon…' but the expansion in the logs really sunk this one for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Looking at this again, the article retains the issues I have raised in my previous correspondence with you.

The inclusion of the final log does little to none to address the more fundamental flaws of the article. Rather, the flaws should be addressed at the bud (via rewriting the bottom half of the article from scratch), not via something included at the end. Ultimately, the added elements make the forced-out nature of the article all the more salient.
